name: Trigonometry — Sine, Cosine and Tangent
whatIsIt: In a right-angled triangle, the three sides have names relative to an angle: OPPOSITE (facing it), ADJACENT (next to it), and HYPOTENUSE (longest). Three ratios connect them: sin = opposite/hypotenuse, cos = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan = opposite/adjacent — remembered as SOH-CAH-TOA. These ratios stay the same for a given angle in ANY similar right triangle, so they let us find unknown sides and heights.
whyNeeded: Trigonometry measures heights and distances you can't reach — a tower, a hillside, a flagpole — using just an angle and one length. It underpins navigation, engineering, waves, and physics.
realLifeUses: Finding a tree or tower's height from an angle and a distance
The steepness (angle) of a ramp or road
The rise of a staircase from its angle

vocabulary.meaning: The side facing the angle / the side next to it (not the hypotenuse).
sin=O/H, cos=A/H, tan=O/A — the three ratios.
A fixed ratio of two sides for a given angle.

story.title: How Tall Is the Tower?
story.text: From 50 m away, Sara measures the angle to the top of a tower as 30°. 'How tall is it?' The engineer says use tangent: tan 30° = height ÷ 50, so height = 50 × tan 30° ≈ 50 × 0.577 ≈ 29 m. 'We found the height without climbing!' Sara marvels. 'That's the power of a ratio.'
story.extractedProblem: From 50 m away, the angle to a tower top is 30°. Roughly how tall is the tower?

visual.caption: Right triangle: sin = opposite/hypotenuse, cos = adjacent/hypotenuse, tan = opposite/adjacent (SOH-CAH-TOA).

teachingGallery.title: Name the sides, pick the ratio
teachingGallery.note: Choose sin, cos or tan by which sides you know/want.

standardMethod.summary: Label the sides relative to the angle, choose the ratio (SOH-CAH-TOA) that links what you know and want, and solve.
steps: Mark the angle; label OPPOSITE (facing it), ADJACENT (beside it), HYPOTENUSE (longest).
Pick the ratio using SOH-CAH-TOA based on which two sides are involved.
Write the equation (e.g. tan θ = opposite/adjacent) and solve for the unknown.
Use a calculator/table for the angle's ratio value; check the answer is reasonable.

workedExamples.problem: A right triangle has opposite 3 and hypotenuse 5. Find sin of the angle.
A right triangle has opposite 4 and adjacent 3. Find tan of the angle.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using sin when the two sides call for tan (mixing up SOH-CAH-TOA).
commonMistakes.fix: Match the ratio to the SIDES: opposite & hypotenuse → sin; adjacent & hypotenuse → cos; opposite & adjacent → tan.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a shorter side the hypotenuse.
commonMistakes.fix: The hypotenuse is always the LONGEST side, opposite the right angle — never one of the sides forming the right angle.

challenge.q: You know the opposite and adjacent sides. Which ratio finds the angle?

options.label: The longest side, opposite the right angle
The side next to the angle

questions.answer: The longest side, opposite the right angle

teachBackPrompt: Draw a right triangle for a grown-up and label opposite, adjacent and hypotenuse for a chosen angle. Explain SOH-CAH-TOA and work out sin, cos and tan for a 3-4-5 triangle (0.6, 0.8, 0.75).

revisionCard.summary: Right triangle sides (for an angle): opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse (longest). SOH-CAH-TOA: sin = O/H, cos = A/H, tan = O/A. Same angle → same ratio in any similar triangle, so ratios find unknown sides, heights and distances.

rememberIt.unpack: Sin = Opposite / Hypotenuse, Cos = Adjacent / Hypotenuse, Tan = Opposite / Adjacent. Say it: 'Some Old Horse Came A-Hopping Through Our Alley.'

studentQuestions.q: How do I know which side is 'opposite' or 'adjacent'?
studentQuestions.a: Stand at the angle. Opposite is the side across from you; adjacent is the side touching you (but not the hypotenuse). The hypotenuse is always the longest, opposite the right angle.
Look at the two sides you have or want. Opp and hyp -> sin. Adj and hyp -> cos. Opp and adj -> tan.
